transcription:
TIM 172A : Introduction to MOT-I :
Management, Development, and Commercialization
(MDC) of New Products and Technologies
Lecture # 1 (9/25/24

        Subhas Desa
1. Instructor , TIM 172A/P, TIM172B/Q, TIM170,
        TIM80C

2. Academic Advisor for all students in
   the Major & Minor.

3. Undergraduate Director of TIM

4. Director (chair) of TIM

   Agenda (for today):

1. Structure and Logic of the course

2. Course Overview / syllabus /operation

3. Technology domains of interest

4. Problem-solving

5. HW # 1

6. Relationship of TIM 172A to TIM 172P
                    subject-matter      project
Note :
1. TIM 172P is only required for the TIM Majors

2. If you are a TIM Major, you need to
enroll in TIM172A and TIM172P during
the same qtr.

3. If you are a TIM Minor, then you
do not enroll in TIM172P
1. Structure of the course:

Motivation:

1. In today's global, highly-competitive
environment in order to be successful (i.e.,
be profitable, satisfy real customer needs, .....)
the company needs to develop

(a) high-quality products (because customers
are more demanding, competition is more
intense, .....)

(b) rapidly, and

(c) at low cost

These are 3 conflicting requirements:

[figure]
2. In addition, the tech. company needs to
be able to successfully manage RISK:
   (a) Technology (Product Development) RISK
   (b) Commercialization (Market) RISK

3. Companies that can successfully manage RISK,
and at the same time develop high-quality
products, rapidly, and at low cost are called
FAST-CYCLE companies

   Examples: Toyota, Intel, Microsoft,
             Google, Apple, Nvidia, Cisco,
             Seagate.

   a few years ago : F A A N G
                     [figure]
   Facebook, Amazon, Apple, Netflix,
                                Google

   and now        : M A N G A
                    [figure]
   Meta Apple Nvidia        same as
                            above

4. The objective of this course, TIM 172A/P,
is to develop the building blocks (tools, techniques,...)
& a framework (or methodology) integrating
these BBs, in order to be a fast-cycle
company or competitor.
2.    Course operation / Syllabus

            Lectures   (SD)                    You

   BBs ⟶ Methods, Tools, ...  }  ⟶  Homework (weekly),
           Framework                  Case Studies,
                                      Midterm, Final
                                      ___________
                                       Take - home
                                          exams

       Home-work cycle:
       You try the problems   complete the HW
       [figure]
       Thurs              Tue          Thurs
                                       HW Solution (perfect)
       Assignment    discuss          from you
       to You        HW in
                     class
Interactions with the TAs:

If you have any issues related to the course.
(e.g., fall ill, grading concern, ....)

please send e-mail to,
    Fariha Lateef (flateef@ucsc.edu)

cc to the instructor (sdesa@ucsc.edu)

Be sure to put an appropriate header in your
message :  TIM 172A .....

Correcting your mistakes in HWs.& Midterm:
Re - submission policy:

1. You have to submit (on-time)

2. After you submission is graded,
   you can correct your mistakes, and
   re-submit, for partial credit: upto 60%
   of your lost grade

3. Work submitted at the end of the
   quarter (last HW set; final exam)
   cannot be re-submitted.
3.    Technology Domains of interest

   (a) IT stack    - Computer Networks (CISCO)
                    - Computer Architecture (Intel)
                    - Operating Systems (Microsoft)
                    - Databases (Oracle)
                    - Application Software
                      (Microsoft, SAP, IBM,....)

   (b) Consumer Electronics
        cell-phones, lap-tops, video-game consoles,
        smart watches, ....

   (c) Smart products
        - household robots, driverless cars,
          smart watches, drones, ....
4.   Problem - Solving

      Complex problems ( the type of problems
      addressed in TIM 172 A/B/P/Q )

         Two examples :

      - What technologies & products should
        company X develop during the next
        1- 3 years ?

      -    How should incoming , real-time data
          (aka "Big Data") be used in order to
          design better products & services
                  ↳ more customer centric, more
                    reliable,....

      How do professionals (in Engineering,
      Business, Medicine, Law) solve complex problems?

      Professionals use a systematic structured
      problem solving process ( SPSP) to solve
      complex problems, which has 5 steps
      as follows :
1. Define the real problem

2. Plan the approach (or treatment) for

solving the problem

3. Execute or implement the plan,
        which leads to
            - Results
            - drawing conclusions
            - making recommendations

4. Check your work/results

5. Learn & Generalize

Each of the above steps has a set of
sub-steps & questions that need to be
answered
            refer to the SPSP handout on CANVAS


5.  . HW # 1
        You are now ready to tackle HW#1

extracted_json.summary_topic_line: Introductory lecture for TIM 172A covering instructor background and the day's agenda.
extracted_json.summary_gist: First lecture (9/25/24) of TIM 172A, Introduction to MOT-I: Management, Development, and Commercialization (MDC) of New Products and Technologies. Lists instructor Subhas Desa's roles (instructor of several TIM courses, academic advisor, undergraduate director, and chair of TIM). The agenda for the day includes course structure and logic, course overview/syllabus/operation, technology domains of interest, problem-solving, HW #1, and the relationship between TIM 172A (subject-matter) and TIM 172P (project). These notes clarify enrollment requirements for TIM 172P. The course is only required for TIM Majors. TIM Majors must enroll in both TIM172A and TIM172P during the same quarter, while TIM Minors do not enroll in TIM172P. Introductory course notes explaining the motivation: in a competitive global environment, a company must develop high-quality products rapidly and at low cost. These three goals (quality, time/speed, cost/efficiency) are conflicting requirements, illustrated by a triangle diagram. Tech companies must manage technology (product development) and commercialization (market) risk. Companies that manage risk while developing high-quality products rapidly and at low cost are called fast-cycle companies (e.g., Toyota, Intel, Microsoft, Google, Apple, Nvidia, Cisco, Seagate; FAANG previously, MANGA now). The course TIM 172A/P aims to develop the building blocks and a framework to become a fast-cycle company. Notes outlining how the course operates. Lectures (SD) cover building blocks (BBs) — methods, tools, and a framework — which feed into student deliverables: weekly homework, case studies, midterm, and final (take-home exams). A timeline shows the homework cycle across two sprints: assignment given Thursday, HW discussed in class Tuesday, and a perfect HW solution due the following Thursday. Students with course issues (illness, grading concerns) should email TA Fariha Lateef and cc the instructor, using a clear "TIM 172A" subject header. The re-submission policy allows correcting graded, on-time submissions for partial credit (up to 60% of lost grade), but end-of-quarter work (the last HW set and final exam) cannot be re-submitted. This page lists three technology domains of interest. (a) The IT stack, broken into layers each with a representative vendor: Computer Networks (CISCO), Computer Architecture (Intel), Operating Systems (Microsoft), Databases (Oracle), and Application Software (Microsoft, SAP, IBM, etc.). (b) Consumer Electronics, with examples such as cell-phones, laptops, video-game consoles, and smart watches. (c) Smart products, including household robots, driverless cars, smart watches, and drones. The page introduces problem-solving for complex problems of the kind addressed in TIM 172A/B/P/Q. Two example problems are given: deciding what technologies and products a company should develop over the next 1-3 years, and how incoming real-time data ("Big Data") can be used to design better, more customer-centric and reliable products and services. It notes that professionals in engineering, business, medicine, and law solve complex problems using a systematic, structured problem solving process (SPSP) consisting of 5 steps. Lists the five steps of a structured problem-solving process: (1) define the real problem, (2) plan the approach/treatment for solving it, (3) execute/implement the plan, which leads to results, drawing conclusions, and making recommendations, (4) check your work/results, and (5) learn & generalize. Notes that each step has sub-steps and questions to answer, directing students to the SPSP handout on Canvas, and states they are now ready to tackle HW#1.
